coredata.dc:title: Nickel ion detection by imidazole modified carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Yan
Liang, Huifeng
coredata.dc:description: Nickel ions (Ni2+) were detected by a new imidazole modified carbon dots (CDs) based on fluorescence quenching method. Structural characterization of CD-imidazole quantum dots were accomplished by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Spectroscopic properties were characterized by ultraviolet absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. Ni2+ concentration showed good linear relationship with fluorescence quenching efficiency in the range of 6–100 mM (R = 0.99) and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.93 mM. Imidazole modified CDs have strong selectivity of Ni2+ compared with other metal ions in aqueous solution. These results demonstrated that the fluorescence sense platform based on imidazole modified CDs can be applied to Ni2+ detection in environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots-imidazole quantum dots
Nickel ions
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD-imidazole and Detection Steps of Ni2+
Synthesis and Characterization of CD-imidazole Quantum Dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of the carbon dots (a) and CD–imidazole (b). The down inset illustrates the HRTEM of the carbon dots (a) and the CD–imidazole solution taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light (b). (B) FTIR spectra of 1H-Imidazole-4-carboxylic acid (a), CDs (b) and CDs imidazole(c). (C) UV–vis spectra of 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid curve (a), the CDs curve (b) and the CD–imidazole curve (c). (D) Time-dependent FL intensity of CD–imidazole.
(A) Time-dependent FL intensity of CD–imidazole upon addition of 2 mM Ni2+. (B) pH-dependent FL quenching efficiency of CD–imidazole upon addition of 2 mM Ni2+.

(A) fluorescence spectra of CD–imidazole in the presence of various concentrations of Ni2+. (B) the relationship between ΔF/F0 and Ni2+. (C) a linear plot was obtained in the concentration range of Ni2+ from 6 mM to 100 mM. (D) The various fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of the CD–imidazole solution in the presence and absence of various individual metal ions.

(A) UV absorption and FL emission spectra of the prepared CD–imidazole QDs. (B) RLS of the CD–imidazole QDs solution containing 0, 0.5 and 1 mM of Ni2+ (from bottom to top). (C) Stern-Volmer plots describes the dependency of the fluorescence intensities on the Ni2+.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Ni2+.
Detection of Ni2+ in synthetic water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nickel ion detection by imidazole modified carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel ions (Ni2+) were detected by a new imidazole modified carbon dots (CDs) based on fluorescence quenching method. Structural characterization of CD-imidazole quantum dots were accomplished by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Spectroscopic properties were characterized by ultraviolet absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. Ni2+ concentration showed good linear relationship with fluorescence quenching efficiency in the range of 6–100 mM (R = 0.99) and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.93 mM. Imidazole modified CDs have strong selectivity of Ni2+ compared with other metal ions in aqueous solution. These results demonstrated that the fluorescence sense platform based on imidazole modified CDs can be applied to Ni2+ detection in environment.

A fluorescence aptasensor switch was developed for Ni2+ detection by imidazole modified carbon dots.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial wastewater is the main pollution source in water environment in China. Among various pollutants in industrial wastewater, heavy metals are one of pollutants which are mostly concerned. Nickel (Ni) is one of heavy metals and it is necessary to make qualitative and quantitative detection of Ni. Ni element is distributed extensively in the nature. There are many Ni detection methods, including EDTA titration method [1], spectrophotometry [2,3], electrochemical process [4], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [5], fluorescent spectrometry [6,7], ICP-AES [8], etc. However, these methods still have some disadvantages, such as tedious biological sample preprocessing and complicated optical devices, expensive machinery and equipments, complicated operation program, or lower LOD.

In the past 20 years, quantum dots have attracted wide attentions of scientific researchers due to the high quantum efficiency, high resistance to photobleaching, long excitation wavelength, narrow emission peak and adjustable emission wavelength [9–14]. However, most of traditional quantum dots are semiconductor nanocrystalline based on metal elements. They not only have high cost and strong toxicity, but also can bring environmental damages [15,16]. The research team of Yaping Sun [17] developed a new carbon nanomaterial for the first time—Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the excellent performance of CQDs has attracted extensive attentions of scientific researchers [17]. CQDs are a type of environmental-friendly fluorescent nanomaterial with similar optical properties of semiconductor quantum dots [18]. With characteristics of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, low preparation cost, easy functional modification and mild reaction conditions [19,20], CQDs have been applied in biomarker and cell imaging [21,22], ion detection [23,24], photoelectric devices [25], photocatalysts [26] and sensors [27–29].

For imidazole, pKa = 7.1. The imidazole with favorable pKa is used as the pH-sensitive functional group in the approximately neutral pH range. Imidazole has strong polarity and thereby improves the hydrophilic properties of CD-imidazole quantum dots. Therefore, the acquired CD-imidazole quantum dots have good water solubility. CD surface was functionalized and endowed with molecular recognition capability. Besides, the quantum dots have good fluorescence properties. The CD-imidazole QDS was used as fluorescence probe to establish the analytical method for Ni2+ detection.

Urea and citric acid anhydrous bp were bought from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Factory. 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid and thionyl chloride were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). Nickel nitrate was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All solvents and reagents were analytical level. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) and ethyl alcohol (EtOH) were dried before the use. High-purified water (18.2 ΩM) in the experiment was prepared by the water purification system WaterPro (Labconco Company, USA).

Morphology of quantum dots was characterized on the 200 kV JEM-2100F TEM (JEOL Company, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was tested on the Varian-660 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Varian Company, USA). Fluorescence spectrum and Resonance Light Scattering (RLS) were recorded in the CaryEclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Company, USA). Ultraviolet visible absorption spectrum was tested by the UV-29100 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Drugs were weighted by the AEU-200 electronic scales (Shimadzu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD-imidazole and Detection Steps of Ni2+
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CD-imidazole is carried out by Xudong Wang research group with some modifications (supplementary material) [30].

The BR buffer solution (pH 6.5, 0.5 ml) and the new prepared CD-imidazole (0.2 mg/ml, 0.1 ml) solution were dissolved into the series 5 ml colorimetric tube. Next, different volumes of standard Ni2+ solutions were added and dissolved into the constant volume. The mixture was reacted by 10 min under room temperature. The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm and the emission wavelength was set 456 nm to test the fluorescence intensity (F). The blank fluorescence intensity of reagents (F0) was tested by the same method. In each detection of Ni2+, the fluorescence measurement was repeated by three times and the average fluorescence signals were acquired. All measurements were performed under room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of CD-imidazole Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-imidazole was synthesis as shown in Scheme 1 . CDs were synthesized by the microwave method in one step by using citric acid and urea as the carbon source. Next, the chlorized imidazole was modified onto CD surface. The CD-imidazole quantum dots were confirmed by TEM and FTIR.

Morphologies of CD (Fig. 1A(a)) and CD-imidazole (Fig. 1A(b)) were characterized by TEM. The TEM demonstrated that CDs were uniformly distributed spherical structures and no significant morphological changes were observed after functionalization of CD surface. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images reflected that the CD diameter was about 5 nm (illustration at lower part of Fig. 1A(a)) and the CD diameter was kept about 5 nm after surface functionalization. Under the 365 nm UV light, CD-imidazole quantum dots solution presented blue-green fluorescence (lower part of Fig. 1A(b)).

In Fig. 1B, the 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid, CD and CD imidazole were verified by the FTIR. The peak in the range of 1442–1575 cm−1 was the skeletal vibration loop (curve a) of the heteroaromatic ring on the 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid. The wide peak at 3431.2 cm−1 was attributed to vibration of amino groups and verified the existence of amino groups on the CD surface. The peak at 1715.3 cm−1 was the characteristic peak of skeletal vibration of heteroaromatic ring on the 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid (curve c). The infrared spectrogram verified that the chlorinated imidazole groups reacted with amino groups on the CD surface to make imidazole modified onto the CD surface successfully.

In Fig. 1C, the UV–visible adsorption spectra of 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid, CD and CD imidazole were studied in BR solution. The peak of 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid at 223 nm was caused by π-π* transition of CN bonds. The absorption spectra of CDs were at 272 nm, 340 nm and 410 nm. The absorption spectra of CD-imidazole had a wide absorption peak at 223 nm, 275 nm and 300–600 nm, indicating that CD-imidazole had the characteristic absorption peaks of 1H-imidazole-4-carboxylic acid and CD. This further proved that imidazole was modified onto CD surface successfully.
In Fig. 1D, the fluorescence intensity of CD-imidazole does not change much within 6 months after it is sealed at the 4 °C, so it can be concluded that CD-imidazole has good optical stability.

To realize Ni2+ sensitive detection, reaction time and pH were optimized. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that Ni2+ (2 mM) was added into CD-imidazole quantum dots. The fluorescence was quenched quickly and reached the equilibrium after 6 min. In the follow-up experiment, 10 min was used as the reaction time of Ni2+ and quantum dots. Additionally, pH of solution influenced quenching effect of CD-imidazole quantum dots. It can be seen from Fig. 2B that the quenching effect increased firstly and then decreased when pH increased from 5.0 to 9.0 and reached the best at pH 6.5. In the follow-up experiments, pH was determined 6.5.

Fig. 3A demonstrated that Ni2+ can quench fluorescence of CD-imidazole quantum dots. With the increase of Ni2+ concentration, fluorescence intensity of CD-imidazole quantum dots declined gradually. When the Ni2+ concentration was 10 mM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached 29%. When the Ni2+ concentration was 80 mM, the fluorescence intensity became very weak and the quenching efficiency reached 61% (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CD-imidazole quantum dots presented a strong linear relationship with Ni2+ concentration in the range of 6–100 mM (R = 0.99) (Fig. 3C). The LOD was 0.93 mM, which was calculated by the standard deviation of 11 measurements based on the 3 times of blank reagents.

Compared with other detection methods in terms of detection limit and detection range (Table 1 ), the detection limit of this method is higher, but the linear range is wider. Compared with colorimetry, this method has higher specificity. In addition, in this sensing system, the synthesis of quantum dots is simpler, the raw materials are cheaper and more environmental friendly and the operation is simpler.

To prove selectivity of the proposed method, effects of potential disturbance ions on CD-imidazole quantum points were tested under the same conditions. For example, concentration of each heavy metal ions, such as Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Li+, Na+ and K+, was 10 mM. By comparing fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CD-imidazole quantum dots solution under the existence and absence of heavy metal ions (Fig. 3D), it found that Ni2+ could quench the fluorescence of quantum dots, without influencing other metal ions significantly. Therefore, CD-imidazole quantum dots could be used for selectivity detection of Ni2+.

To evaluate feasibility of CD-imidazole quantum dots fluorescence probe in actual Ni2+ detection, Ni2+ in three synthesis samples was tested by the same method. All three samples were prepared by different volumes of Ni2+ standard solution into tap water. Results are shown in Table 2 . The recovery rates of standard adding method were 102.27%, 84.97% and 95.93%, respectively. RSD was smaller than 4%, indicating the high accuracy of the constructed probe. These experimental results demonstrated that this method has potential application values for Ni2+ detection in the environment.

Influence of metal ions on fluorescence quenching of quantum dots is a complicated process. Generally speaking, the quenching mechanism includes fluorescence resonance energy transfer, non-radiative composite pathway, electronic transfer process and ion interaction [34,35]. Based on comparison of absorption spectrum of Ni2+ (Fig. 4A) and emission spectrum of quantum dots, the possibility of energy transfer between Ni2+ and quantum dots could be eliminated. The reaction capability between Ni2+ and acyl groups was weaker than amino groups. As a result, the added Ni2+ would corrode quantum dot surface, thus eliminating the possibility of forming a new non-radiative surface channel basically.

In addition, shapes of scattering spectrum of the quantum dot solution (Fig. 4B) remained same after different volumes of Ni2+ standard solution were added. However, the scattering spectral intensity of quantum dot solution was increased significantly, indicating that the clustering states of quantum dots in the solution changed. This revealed that Ni2+ and CD-imidazole quantum dots reacted and formed complexes. This paper was attempted to construct the Stern-Volmer relationship (F0/F and [Ni2+]) (Fig. 4C). The Stern-Volmer diagram presented a linear relationship when Ni2+ concentration was 0–10 mM and 20–100 mM. This implied that the charge transfer between CD-imidazole dot and Ni2+ might be a dynamic mechanism.
To sum up, the fluorescence quenching of CD-imidazole dots by Ni2+ might be achieved by dynamically bonded photoinduction electron transfer mechanism.

A new CD-imidazole nanoprobe is developed by binding imidazole onto the CD surface. The generated CDs have high water solubility, specificity, fluorescence and light resistance. They are hardly disturbed by other metal ions in aqueous solution. The fluorescence intensity of CD-imidazole dots has a good linear relationship with Ni2+ concentration in the range of 6–100 mM (R = 0.99) and the LOD is 0.93 mM. Results demonstrate that CD-imidazole quantum dots can be applied to Ni2+ detection in environment. Moreover, CD-imidazole quantum dots possess promising application prospects in nano sensor, biocatalyst and cell imaging.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 31700876, 31700862), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant no. 201601D021106), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi Normal University (Grant no. ZR1602).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.12.024.
